18: A direct revelation: all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me. The post-resurrection commissioning is the Danielic enthronement realized: the Son of Man to whom all authority was given (Daniel 7:14) now speaks in his own name. The crucified Jesus is the risen and enthroned Lord — his death was not the end of his authority but the means by which the authority was exercised and the path by which it was received. The Great Commission is grounded in this universal authority: he sends because he has been given the power to send, and his presence accompanies the sent ones because his authority extends to every place they go.
20: A direct revelation: I am with you always, to the end of the age. The Gospel that opened with Immanuel — God with us — closes with the personal promise of the same presence: I am with you. The Shekinah-theology of the OT (YHWH dwelling with his people in the tabernacle, the temple, the cloud of fire) reaches its final form in the risen Christ's personal, perpetual, universal accompaniment of his community. The age of the Spirit is not a lesser presence but the continuation of the Immanuel-presence through the risen body of the ascended Son, who is both seated at the right hand and present with his people through the Spirit he pours out.
1: A direct revelation: "Now after the Sabbath, toward the dawn of the first day of the week, Mary Magdalene and the other Mary went to see the tomb." The first day of the week — the day after the Sabbath — is the Christological new-creation day. In Genesis, day one is the day of light. The women who go to see the tomb at the first light of the first day are approaching the event that begins the new creation. The resurrection on the first day of the week (not the seventh) is the beginning of the new Genesis.
2: A direct revelation: "And behold, there was a great earthquake, for an angel of the Lord descended from heaven and came and rolled back the stone and sat on it." The earthquake at the resurrection (as at the crucifixion, Matthew 27:51) is the Christological cosmic sign: the earth shakes at the death and at the resurrection of the Son. The angel rolling the stone is not to let Jesus out — he has already risen — but to show that the tomb is empty. The Christological revelation: the stone is rolled away not to release the risen Lord but to display the vacated tomb.
3: A revelation of God: "His appearance was like lightning, and his clothing white as snow." The angel whose appearance is like lightning and whose clothing is white as snow is described in the language of divine theophany (Daniel 10:6; Ezekiel 1:14). The Christological frame: the messenger who announces the resurrection has the appearance of the heavenly realm to which the risen Son has returned. The resurrection is a heavenly event that breaks into the earthly realm, and the messenger reflects that heavenly origin.
4: A revelation of God: "And for fear of him the guards trembled and became like dead men." The guards who were stationed to prevent the resurrection became "like dead men" at the angel's presence. The Christological irony: the men stationed to guard the dead become like dead men themselves; the dead man they guarded is alive. The power that felled the guards is the power of the risen Lord's resurrection reality breaking into the world through the angelic announcement.
5: A direct revelation: "But the angel said to the women, 'Do not be afraid, for I know that you seek Jesus who was crucified.'" The angel's greeting — "do not be afraid" — is the Christological comfort of every divine encounter (cf. Matthew 1:20; Matthew 14:27; Matthew 17:7). The specification "Jesus who was crucified" is the Christological identification: the risen one is identified by the cross, not despite it. The crucified one is the one who is risen; the resurrection does not erase the crucifixion but vindicates it.
6: A direct revelation: "He is not here, for he has risen, as he said. Come, see the place where he lay." The Easter proclamation — "he is not here, for he has risen" — is the Christological announcement that is the fulcrum of the Gospel and of human history. "As he said" confirms every passion prediction: Matthew 16:21; Matthew 17:23; Matthew 20:19; Matthew 26:32 — all fulfilled in this moment. The invitation "come, see the place where he lay" is the Christological evidence: the empty tomb is the first physical sign of the resurrection.
7: A direct revelation: "Then go quickly and tell his disciples that he has risen from the dead, and behold, he is going before you to Galilee; there you will see him. See, I have told you." The "going before you to Galilee" fulfills Matthew 26:32 (the promise made at the Last Supper: "after I am raised, I will go before you to Galilee"). The Christological re-gathering: the scattered sheep (Matthew 26:31; Zechariah 13:7) will be gathered by the risen shepherd who goes before them. The commission to tell the disciples is the first post-resurrection missionary mandate — given to women.
8: A revelation of God: "So they departed quickly from the tomb with fear and great joy, and ran to tell his disciples." The mixture of "fear and great joy" is the Christological emotional reality of the resurrection: the awe before the divine event and the overwhelming gladness of the disciples' Lord being alive. The running is the urgency of the gospel: the best news in human history cannot be delivered slowly. The women are the first evangelists of the resurrection.
9: A direct revelation: "And behold, Jesus met them and said, 'Greetings!' And they came up and took hold of his feet and worshiped him." The first resurrection appearance in Matthew is to the women. The greeting (chairete, "rejoice!") is the Christological announcement of the joy the resurrection brings. The holding of his feet is the physical confirmation of bodily resurrection — they touch him; he is real. The worship is the immediate response to the risen Lord's presence: the same posture as the Magi (Matthew 2:11), Peter and the disciples on the water (Matthew 14:33), the Transfiguration disciples (Matthew 17:6).
10: A direct revelation: "Then Jesus said to them, 'Do not be afraid; go and tell my brothers to go to Galilee, and there they will see me.'" The "do not be afraid" of the risen Lord is the Christological continuation of every "fear not" in the Gospel (Matthew 1:20; Matthew 14:27; Matthew 17:7; Matthew 28:5). "My brothers" — the word Jesus uses for the disciples after the resurrection — is the Christological adoption: the one who came to be the Son has made the disciples brothers through his death and resurrection (cf. John 20:17: "go to my brothers and say to them, I am ascending to my Father and your Father"). The family language of the resurrection is the new relationship constituted by the cross.
11: A revelation of God: "While they were going, behold, some of the guard went into the city and told the chief priests all that had taken place." The guards' report to the chief priests — rather than to Pilate — is the Christological political choice: they report to those who hired them, who will manage the situation. The ones who were stationed to prevent the resurrection become the inadvertent witnesses to it; their report to the chief priests is the first human account of the resurrection events.
12: A revelation of God: "And when they had assembled with the elders and taken counsel, they gave a sufficient sum of money to the soldiers." The bribery of the soldiers is the Christological sequel to the bribery of Judas (Matthew 26:15): both betrayals of the Son are transacted with money. The Sanhedrin that paid thirty pieces of silver to have him killed now pays a "sufficient sum" to cover up his resurrection. The passion narrative begins and ends with monetary conspiracy against the truth about Jesus.
13: A revelation of God: "Saying, 'Tell people, 'His disciples came by night and stole him away while we were asleep.''" The fabricated story — disciples stealing the body at night — is the Christological cover-up that the narrative has already dismantled. The sealed tomb, the guards, the sealed stone: all make the theft story implausible. And the guards who were "asleep" could not know what happened if they were asleep. The lie is self-refuting, but it will be repeated "to this day" (Matthew 28:15).
14: A revelation of God: "And if this comes to the governor's ears, we will satisfy him and keep you out of trouble." The promise to handle Pilate is the Christological institutional protection of the cover-up: the same Sanhedrin that manipulated Pilate into the crucifixion now promises to manage him again if necessary. The Christological bearing: every human power that conspires against the resurrection — political, religious, military — is unable to prevent the resurrection's proclamation.
15: A revelation of God: "So they took the money and did as they were directed. And this story has been spread among the Jews to this day." The perpetuation of the false narrative "to this day" (at the time of Matthew's writing) is the Christological acknowledgment that the resurrection will always be contested. The gospel is not proclaimed into a vacuum but against a competing story. The Christological confidence: the evidence for the resurrection is stronger than the story invented to deny it, and the church has been proclaiming the risen Lord in the face of the denial since the first morning.
16: A revelation of God: "Now the eleven disciples went to Galilee, to the mountain to which Jesus had directed them." The eleven on the mountain in Galilee is the Christological re-gathering of the scattered disciples: the shepherd struck (Matthew 26:31; Zechariah 13:7) has risen and gathered the flock. The mountain in Galilee — the region of the ministry's beginning — is the setting for the final commissioning. The same geography that saw the Sermon on the Mount (Matthew 5:1) and the Transfiguration (Matthew 17:1) now receives the risen Lord's commission.
17: A revelation of God: "And when they saw him they worshiped him, but some doubted." The worship of the risen Lord combined with "some doubted" (hoi de edistasan) is the Christological honesty of Matthew's resurrection account. The doubt is not denied or suppressed — it is recorded alongside the worship. The Christological reality of the resurrection community: genuine encounter with the risen Lord does not eliminate every doubt, but the doubt does not prevent the mission. Jesus commissions the ones who doubted as well as those who worshiped.
19: A direct revelation: "Go therefore and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit." The Great Commission is the Christological realization of the mustard seed parable (Matthew 13:31-32) and the Isaiah servant songs (Isaiah 42:6; Matthew 49:6: "a light for the nations"): what was limited in Jesus' earthly ministry (Matthew 10:5-6: "go nowhere among the Gentiles") is now universal. The baptism "in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit" is the Trinitarian formula that identifies the risen Son with the Father and Spirit in a single divine name. The Son who was revealed as the Father's Son is now identified as the second person of the Triune God.
